segments.s691-g137: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. دیده is the noun 'sight' (not a participle here) and خواهی is the second-person singular present/future of خواستن, so دیده خواهی = 'you want (to have) sight' or 'if you wish to see'. ندارم is 'I do not have' (present), followed by the noun دریغ 'reluctance/pity' — literally 'I have no reluctance'. The pronouns are omitted as usual in Persian (ندارم = I do not have).
segments.s691-g138: که here functions as a conjunction meaning 'for' or 'because' and introduces the reason. دیده again is the noun 'sight' (subject of the comparative). به از is a classical comparative idiom meaning 'better than' (modern Persian: بهتر از), so دیده به از X = 'sight is better than X'. The list گنج، دینار و تیغ names the things compared (treasure, dinars, and the sword).
segments.s691-g89: که is the subordinating conjunction 'that'. در پیش + noun forms a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of / before'; here در (in) + پیش (presence of) + قیصر (Caesar) => 'before Caesar'. بیارم is an archaic/poetic verbal form here (not the modern noun senses listed) functioning as part of the verb complex; نشست is the past tense 'sat/took a seat' (third-person singular) and completes the clause 'I/one sat before Caesar'.
segments.s691-g90: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying the following phrase. نامه شاه ایران is a genitive construction 'the letter of the King of Iran' (نامه 'letter' + شاه 'king' + ایران 'Iran'). بدست here is an adverbial/completing expression 'into the hand / into possession' — 'came into his hand' (not the instrumental 'by' reading).
segments.s691-g91: مگر here introduces an exception or condition 'unless'. بندگی را is the direct object 'servitude' with the definite/object marker را. پسند + آیمت together express 'to please / to be approved' in an archaic/poetic verbal construction: پسند is the notion of approval, and آیمت is an older/poetic verb form (optative/subjunctive-like) meaning 'you will be / you will come to be' or 'it will be so'. The clause therefore reads 'unless (you) find/accept servitude pleasing.'
segments.s691-g92: به پیغام او is a prepositional phrase 'by/through his message' (به = 'by/through/to', پیغام = 'message', او = 'his/he'). سودمند is an adjective 'useful/profitable' describing the result, and again آیمت is the same archaic/poetic verb form meaning 'you will be / you will prove to be'. The clause means 'by his message you will be useful' (or 'his message will make you useful').
segments.s691-g93: بدو is the prepositional/clitic phrase 'to him' (short form of به + او). گفت here is the past tense verb 'said' (third-person) introducing reported speech. قیصر is the speaker 'Caesar'. که introduces the content clause 'that...' and بگشای is an imperative/optative form 'open/reveal' (the present-stem imperative form) taking راز 'secret' as its object: 'Reveal the secret.'
segments.s691-g94: چه is the interrogative 'what' introducing a question. گفت here functions as the past-tense verb 'said' (what was said). آن خردمند گردن‌فراز is the noun phrase 'that proud-wise man' (آن = that/the, خردمند = wise man, گردن‌فراز = proud/boastful), so the clause asks 'what did that proud wise man say?'.
segments.s691-g95: نخست is an adverb 'first/at first'. آفرین here is a noun 'praise/acclamation' directed بر جهاندار 'to the sovereign' (بر + جهاندار = 'to/upon the ruler'). کرد is the past tense 'did/uttered/made' and completes the verbal clause 'he gave praise to the sovereign.'
segments.s691-g96: جهان را is the direct object 'the world' with the object marker را. بدان is a fused/elliptical prepositional phrase 'by that' (ب + آن) referring back to the previous praise. آفرین again is 'praise', and خوارکرد is a compound verb 'to humiliate / to make low' (خوار + کرد). Together: 'by that praise he made the world low / humbled the world.'
segments.s691-g17: بخراد here is an archaic/poetic epithet meaning 'the wise' (not the verb 'buy') and precedes the proper names as a descriptive title; برزین و شاپور are two coordinated personal names, and شیر (lit. 'lion') is an epithet attached to شاپور (Shapur-Shīr). The whole phrase is a nominative/vocative group that names the addressees of the following speech.
segments.s691-g18: چنین گفت is a fixed poetic formula 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت here represented as the speech-verb formula); پس here is a temporal/connecting adverb 'then' and شهریار دلیر is the subject noun phrase 'the brave king' following the verb in the emphatic, archaic word order typical of Ferdowsi.
segments.s691-g19: که introduces the content clause (here rendered as 'when/that'), اسپان is the plural 'horses', چو روشن شود is a temporal subordinate clause 'when they become bright' (چو = 'when', روشن = 'bright/ready', شود = present/subjunctive/become), and زین کنید is an imperative (2pl) 'make/prepare saddles' — the verb comes after its object in this poetic ordering.
segments.s691-g20: ببالای (ب + بالا) is a prepositional/locative compound 'on/above', آن = 'that/it', followed by the object زین 'saddle' modified by زرین 'golden'; کنید is again the 2nd-person-plural imperative 'place/make' — together: 'place a golden saddle on that.'
segments.s691-g21: بپوشید is the 2nd-person-plural imperative 'put on/wear'; its direct object follows: زربفت 'gold-woven, brocaded' and چینی 'Chinese' both function as adjectival modifiers of قبای 'robe(s)'. Word order (verb + modifiers + noun) is typical of Ferdowsi's elevated style.
segments.s691-g22: همه 'all/everyone' is the addressee; یک دلانید is a predicative/optative (2pl) form meaning 'be of one heart' or 'be united' (an exhortation), و 'and' links the clauses, پاکیزه رای is a nominal phrase 'pure/clean counsel' (پاکیزه = 'pure/clean', رای = 'counsel/opinion'), so the clause urges unity and purity of counsel.
segments.s691-g23: ازین ('from this') is the prepositional phrase marking origin (از + این); شارستان = 'city/town'; سوی + قیصر = 'toward the Caesar'; شوید is a 2nd-person-plural optative/imperative form (lit. 'become/go') here used to command movement — 'go to the Caesar from this city.'
segments.s691-g24: بگویید is an imperative (2pl) 'speak/say' (i.e. address him), و 'and' links the actions, گفتار او 'his speech/words' is the object phrase, and بشنوید is an imperative (2pl) 'hear/listen' — together: 'speak and listen to what he says.'
segments.s691-g73: رسیدند is the simple past, 3rd-person plural ‘they arrived/approached’. نزدیک is an adverb meaning ‘near’ and modifies رسیدند. قیصر فراز is a noun plus an epithet: قیصر 'the Caesar/emperor' and فراز as an epithet ‘on high/exalted’ (postpositive adjective-like), so the whole segment means ‘they came near the exalted Caesar’.
segments.s691-g74: چو here is the conjunction 'when'. دیدند is past tense 3pl 'they saw'. بردند is past 3pl of بردن 'they carried/brought'. پیشش is a prepositional/adverbial form meaning 'before him / in his presence' (پیش + enclitic pronoun -ش). نماز is used as the object meaning 'prayer(s)' or 'obeisance' that they present. The clause as a whole: 'when they saw him, they brought/presented their prayers before him.'
segments.s691-g75: همه 'all/everyone' is the subject. یک‌زبان is a frozen compound 'of one tongue' = unanimously. آفرین is an interjection/noun 'bravo' or acclamation. خواندند is past 3pl 'they uttered/sang/said' — here used to indicate they exclaimed together. Together: 'All, with one voice, cried “afarin.”'
segments.s691-g76: بران is a contraction meaning 'upon that' (on/at that). تخت is 'throne' (the location receiving the action). زر گوهر is a compound of two nouns 'gold and jewels' (direct objects). افشاندند is past 3pl of افشاندن 'they scattered/sprinkled/strewn' and takes زر گوهر as its object: 'they scattered gold and jewels upon that throne.'
segments.s691-g77: نخستین 'first' is an adverb/adjective indicating order. بپرسید is past/formal past 3sg or classical form 'asked' (here narrative past: 'he asked'). قیصر is the subject 'Caesar'. ز is the poetic preposition 'about/from' linking پرسید to its topic (z + noun): 'Caesar first asked of the king' (i.e., posed a question to the king).
segments.s691-g78: از ایران begins a list of topics with the preposition از 'about/concerning' (here marking the object/topic of asking). وز is actually the fused sequence و + ز ('and of') introducing the next topic, but the provided lemma senses do not reflect that function (see senses). لشکر 'army' is the next topic. و 'and' continues the list. رنج راه is a compound meaning 'the hardship(s) of the road/journey' (رنج 'hardship' governed by راه 'road'). Overall: 'about Iran, and about the army and the hardships of the road.'
segments.s691-g79: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is past 3sg 'he heard'. خراد برزین is a personal name (Kharad Barzin); syntactically this is the agent who heard. برفت is a past tense verb meaning 'he went (up / forth)' in classical/narrative Persian. The whole: 'When Kharad Barzin heard (this), he went.'
segments.s691-g80: برتخت is a locative/adverbial compound 'to/up on the throne' (بر + تخت). با is the instrumental/with preposition 'with'. نامه شاه 'the king's letter' — نامه is direct object/instrument and شاه is its genitive possessor. تفت in the given senses does not match the apparent narrative sense here; the intended reading is simply the remainder of the phrase describing the letter or occasion, but with the provided senses no exact lexical sense is found.
segments.s691-g57: This is a compact verbal clause: the prepositional phrase 'به نزدیک قیصر' (toward/near the Caesar) precedes the past‑tense verb 'نهادند' (they placed/set) and is followed by the noun 'روی' (face). In Persian the idiom 'روی نهادن به X' means 'to turn one's face toward X' (i.e. to face/approach X), so the literal order here is 'toward the Caesar they set (their) face.'
segments.s691-g58: This is a noun phrase describing those who acted: 'بزرگان' (the great ones) modified by two adjectival/compound attributes 'روشن‌دل' (clear‑hearted/bright‑minded) and 'راست گوی' (literally 'true‑speaking' = truthful). Word order is noun + modifiers in apposition; 'و' simply links the two descriptive terms.
segments.s691-g59: 'چو' is the temporal conjunction 'when,' and 'بشنید' is the simple past 'he heard' (3rd person). The relative/compound particle 'کز' introduces the content of what was heard ('that ...'), followed by 'ایران مهان' (nobles/visitors from Iran). The clause is elliptical and poetic — the reported content continues in the next segment.
segments.s691-g60: Here 'فرستاده' functions as a past participle/verb form 'sent' (not as the noun 'messenger'), and 'شهریار جهان' is the agent phrase 'the king/lord of the world.' In Persian poetic order the participle can precede its agent, so the whole segment completes the content of what the Caesar heard: '(that) the sovereign of the world had sent (them).'
segments.s691-g61: 'رسیدند' is the past tense 3rd‑person plural 'they arrived/came.' The locative phrase 'نزدیک ایوان' ('near the iwan/porch') follows, and the instrumental/route phrase 'ز راه' ('by/from the road' = along the road) closes the clause. Word order (verb — locative) is typical of narrative Persian poetry.
segments.s691-g62: In this clause 'پذیره' (reception) is used adverbially/nominally ('for the reception' or 'to receive them'), followed by the past verb 'فرستاد' ('he sent'). The object 'چندی سپاه' ('several troops') is placed after the verb: literally, '(for) reception he sent several troops' = 'he dispatched several soldiers to meet/receive them.'
segments.s691-g63: 'بیاراست' is a past (perfective) verb 'he adorned' (3rd person). The direct object 'کاخی' ('a palace') follows, and the instrument/quality phrase 'به دیبای روم' indicates the material/style ('in/by Roman brocade'). The preposition 'به' is used here instrumentally/qualitatively in poetic diction.
segments.s691-g64: This is an equational/descriptive clause: 'همه' ('entirely/all') modifies 'پیکرش' ('its figure/body'), and the predicate 'گوهر و زر بوم' lists what it consisted of — 'gems and the gold of the land.' Word order is subject ('همه پیکرش') then predicate ('گوهر و زر بوم').
segments.s691-g121: مرا اندرین کار یاری کنید is an imperative request. مرا (م + را) is the accusative object 'me' (colloquially cliticized here) followed by the object marker را; اندرین کار = 'in this affair/undertaking' (an old/poetic compound: اندر + این). یاری کنید is a polite plural imperative (or hortative) from کردن + یاری, literally 'do help' = 'help'. Word order is object — adjunct — verb (typical Persian: [object] [adverbial phrase] [verb]).
segments.s691-g122: برین بی‌وفا کامگاری کنید is a second clause continuing the command. برین is orthographic contraction of بر این ('against/on this' or 'with respect to this'); بی‌وفا = 'unfaithful/disloyal' describes the person; کامگاری کنید is again an imperative meaning 'bring about success' or 'secure success'. The verb is causative/actional 'do' (کنید) applied to کامگاری 'success/achievement'.
segments.s691-g123: که پوینده گشتیم گرد جهان begins with the subordinating که introducing an explanatory clause ('that' / 'for'). پوینده is a participial/agentive form ('one who seeks' / 'wanderer'); گشتیم is the past (perfective) 'we became' or 'we turned' (first-person plural). گرد جهان is a postpositional phrase 'around/about the world' (گرد = around). The clause says 'that we became wanderers roaming the world.'
segments.s691-g124: بشرم آمدیم از کهان ومهان continues the thought: بشرم ('we humans' — an inclusive noun phrase) آمدیم 'we came' (perfective past). از کهان ومهان is a poetic origin phrase 'from that which is old/primeval and from the moon/month' — here functioning as a genitive/source introduced by از. The clause gives a broad, poetic provenance of humankind.
segments.s691-g125: چوقیصر بران سان سخنها شنید is a simple past clause: چوقیصر 'like Caesar' (a simile/title), بران سان = 'on/at that (matter), in that manner' (بران + سان = 'thereupon / in that way'), سخنها 'the words/speeches' (plural), شنید is the past tense 'he heard'. Word order: topical nominative — locative/adverbial — object — verb.
segments.s691-g126: برخساره شد چون گل شنبلید is idiomatic: برخساره شد = 'fell into loss/be ruined' (بر + خساره with شد 'became'), an intransitive perfective construction meaning he was overwhelmed or ruined. چون + simile follows: 'like the fenugreek-flower' (گل شنبلید). The verbless simile is linked by شد ('became'), so 'he became, like the fenugreek-flower.'
segments.s691-g127: گل شنبلیدش پر از ژاله شد is a nominal phrase + copula: گل شنبلیدش = 'its fenugreek-flower' (گل + شنبلید + -ش possessive), پر از ژاله = 'full of dew/tears' (پر از + noun), شد = 'became/was'. The construction describes a bodily/poetic image: the flower (metaphorically an eye or face-part) was filled with drops.
segments.s691-g128: زبان و روانش پر ازناله شد is parallel: زبان و روانش 'his tongue and his soul' (two coordinated noun phrases, روانش bears the possessive -ش), پر از ناله 'full of lamentation/complaint' (پر از + noun), شد 'became' — describing inner and outer sorrow. Note the poetic concatenation of ازناله as a compound.
segments.s691-g9: همه روم is the subject phrase 'all of Rome / all the Romans' (همه = 'all', روم = 'the Romans/Byzantium'). یکسر is an adverb meaning 'entirely/altogether' modifying the predicate. تو را uses را as the emphatic/definite object marker attached to the second-person pronoun (classical word order places the object marked with را before the verb). کهترند is a present-tense plural predicate 'are inferior' (ند ending = 3rd-person plural).
segments.s691-g10: اگر چند begins a concessive clause 'even if / though a few' (اگر = 'if/even if', چند = 'some/a few'). گردنکش is an attributive noun/adjective 'rebellious, headstrong' describing those few. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and', and مهترند is the clause-final predicate 'they are greater/chiefs' (present-tense plural). The whole clause concedes that a few may be bold or prominent, without overturning the previous claim.
segments.s691-g11: تو را is the emphatic object 'you' (را marks the direct object). تا introduces a temporal/subordinating clause 'until'. نسازم is first-person singular present/subjunctive (classical form) 'I make / I have made' in the sense 'until I make/prepare'. سلیح و سپاه are the objects of نسازم (sلیح = 'armament/arms', سپاه = 'army'), placed after the verb of the subordinate clause; the main negated verb (in the following segment) depends on this 'until' clause.
segments.s691-g12: نجویم is the first-person present with the negative prefix ن- here meaning 'I will not seek / I shall not look for'. خور و خواب و آرام گاه are the coordinated objects: خور (here contextually 'food/eating' as object of seeking), خواب 'sleep', and آرام گاه a compound 'place/time of rest' ('آرام' = rest/quiet, گاه = place/time). Word order places the negated verb before its objects in this classical line.
segments.s691-g13: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is the simple past 'he heard'. خسرو is the subject 'Khosrow / the king' (classical Persian allows subject after the verb). بدان is a short compound meaning 'of that / at that (news)' (ب- as prepositional prefix + an demonstrative), and شاد گشت is the past-tense result 'became happy' (گشت = 'became').
segments.s691-g14: روانش = 'his mind / his spirit' with the possessive suffix -ش. از اندیشه is a prepositional phrase 'from (his) thought/worry' (از = 'from', اندیشه = 'thought/concern'). آزاد گشت is the past result 'became free' (گشت = 'became'), so the clause means his mind was relieved/free from anxious thought.
segments.s691-g15: بفرمود is an honorific past-tense verb 'he said/commanded' (polite 3rd-person). گستهم و بالوی are proper names (two men being named) placed immediately after the verb as the objects of the order; را is the definite object marker attached to the coordinated names. In classical Persian the verb may precede the marked object phrase.
segments.s691-g16: همان is a demonstrative 'the same / those very'. اندیان here functions as a plural noun 'those men/people' (classical formation; the precise lexical entry given in the list does not match exactly, so sense is marked as uncertain). جهانجوی is an attributive noun 'world-seeking, ambitious (ones)' describing those men. را again marks the whole phrase as the object or referent of the previous command — 'those same ambitious, world-seeking men.'
segments.s691-g33: This is a reported-command structure: بفرمود شاه is the main clause 'the king commanded/said' (بفرمود = honorific past of گفتن/فرمودن). The preceding phrase بخراد برزین is a subordinate jussive/subjunctive clause ('that Barzin buy' / 'that Barzin should buy') placed before the main verb, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s691-g34: که introduces the content of the king's command ('that...'). چینی حریرآر و مشک سیاه lists the objects ordered: چینی (adjective/noun 'Chinese') modifies حریرآر ('silk-adorned, silken'), and then و links to مشک سیاه 'black musk'. Word order is noun/adjective compounds typical of poetic Persian.
segments.s691-g35: This is a necessity construction: باید + infinitive (نوشت) expresses obligation ('one must write' / 'a letter must be written'). The indirect object به قیصر ('to Caesar') and the indefinite one/one-letter یکی نامه precede باید + نوشت for emphasis; word order (preposing the goal) is normal in Persian for focus.
segments.s691-g36: چو introduces a comparative/hypothetical clause ('as/like' or 'when'); خورشید تابان is a nominal phrase 'the shining sun'. بخرم is first-person present/subjunctive with the prefix ب- ('I may buy' / 'I will buy' in narrative/poetic diction) and بهشت is the object 'paradise'. The clause reads as a simile or hypothetical: 'I will buy paradise like the shining sun.'
segments.s691-g37: سخنهای کوتاه = 'short/brief sayings' (plural + adjective). و links to معنی بسی = 'much/abundant meaning' (معنی 'meaning' + بسی 'much/ample'). The phrase lists desirable qualities (brevity and depth) in parallel.
segments.s691-g38: که introduces a purposive/result clause: آن یاد گیرد دل هر کسی = 'that that remembrance may seize (گیرد) the heart of every person.' گیرد is present/subjunctive/optative here ('may take/seize'), and دل هر کسی is the affected possessor phrase 'the heart of each person'.
segments.s691-g39: که again introduces a subordinate clause: نزدیک او فیلسوفان بوند = 'philosophers were/should be near him.' بوند is the classical/poetic past/optative plural form of بودن ('they were' / 'they should be'). Word order places the locative/adjunct and then the subject and the copula in a literary arrangement.
segments.s691-g40: بدان کوش is an exhortation 'strive at/attend to that' (بدان = 'in/at that', کوش = imperative/infinitive 'strive'). تا introduces purpose ('so that') and is followed by the negative subjunctive نشنوند ('they do not hear' / 'may they not hear'), giving the aim: 'strive so that they hear no nonsense' (یاوه‌ای = 'any nonsense'). Note نشنوند is 3rd-person plural negative subjunctive/jussive.
segments.s691-g97: که introduces a relative clause here meaning 'who/that'. اویست is an archaic/poetic form best read as او + است ('he is') functioning as the clause's copula ('is'); it is not acting as a proper name in this context. برتر زهر برتری is a compact predicate: برتر 'superior' modifies the following nominal phrase (زهر برتری), where زهر is used figuratively ('poison/malice') and برتری restates 'preeminence'.
segments.s691-g98: توانا و داننده is a coordinate description: توانا 'capable/powerful' and داننده 'one who knows' (a present-participle adjective). از هر دری is a prepositional phrase ('from/of every door/field') modifying داننده; دری here means 'domain/field' (not one of the listed senses), so the preposition از carries its usual 'from/out of' sense.
segments.s691-g99: بفرمان او is an ablative/agentive phrase 'by the command of him' (بـ + فرمان + object 'او'). گردد is the present-subjunctive/archaic third-person form of گشت/گردیدن here meaning 'turns/is made to turn' or more generally 'happens', and it applies to the following subject این آسمان 'this heaven/heavenly sphere'. Word order is emphatic: the agent phrase precedes the verb and the subject.
segments.s691-g100: کجا introduces a rhetorical/exclamatory question 'where/how' (here: 'how could it be that...'). برترست is a fused adjective+copula meaning 'is superior'. از مکان و زمان is the comparative complement 'to/from place and time' (از expresses the standard of comparison). The conjunction و links مکان 'place' and زمان 'time'.
segments.s691-g101: سپهر و ستاره همه کرده‌اند is a nominal subject (سرپرست) 'the heavens and the stars' plus the perfect auxiliary کرده‌اند 'they have done/made', with همه adding 'all/altogether' for emphasis. The verb is perfective/resultative describing a completed action by these celestial bodies.
segments.s691-g102: بدین is a prepositional unit (به + این) meaning 'to/onto this'; here it modifies چرخ گردان 'the revolving wheel/sphere' which is a compound where چرخ is 'wheel/sphere' and گردان is the participial adjective 'revolving'. برآورده‌اند is a perfect plural verb meaning 'they have brought/placed/raised' (literal 'brought up' or 'set in place').
segments.s691-g103: چو functions here as a temporal conjunction 'when'. از خاک introduces the material/source 'from the earth/dust'. مر is an archaic object pronoun 'me' (so the clause literally says 'from the earth he made me an animal/creature'), جانور 'creature/animal' and بنده 'servant/subject' combine as a predicate 'made (me) an animal/servant', and کرد is the past tense verb 'made/did'. The word order (source → object → predicate → verb) is typical of classical Persian narrative.
segments.s691-g104: نخستین is an ordinal/adverb 'first' modifying the verb phrase that follows. کیومرث را marks 'Kiumars' as the direct object with the definite/object marker را. زنده کرد is a causative perfect/past 'brought to life' or 'made alive' (زنده 'alive' + کرد 'made'). The clause states that the first one to be enlivened was Kiumars.
segments.s691-g113: کنون is an adverb meaning “now” placed at the front for temporal setting; بنده‌ای is indefinite (بنده + ـی) “a servant” with the postpositive ـای marking indefiniteness; ناسزاوار is an adjectival description “abusive / foul‑mouthed” placed after the noun (Persian order: noun + adjective); وگست is an archaic connective (‘and then’ / ‘moreover’) linking this descriptive phrase to the following clause.
segments.s691-g114: بیامد is a past/perfective verb “came”; بتخت is the prepositional phrase به + تخت written together here and functions as the goal “to/on the throne” (treated as one unit in the line); کیان names the royal house (the Kayanids) in genitive relation to تخت; برنشست is the past form of نشستن with the prefix بر‑ meaning “sat upon / took a seat on.” The sequence places the verbs after the subject phrase as is normal in Persian narrative.
segments.s691-g115: همی‌داد is a classical/formal verbal phrase with همی‑ indicating continued or habitual action (here rendered as a giving/doing form); خواهم is the first‑person auxiliary/modal ‘I will / I shall’ that gives a future or volitional sense to the clause; ز is the poetic form of the preposition ‘from’; بیدادگر is the agent ‘the tyrant / oppressor.’ Together the phrase expresses a volition about action directed at or arising from the oppressor.
segments.s691-g116: نه ... نه ... is the correlative negation ‘neither ... nor ...’ listing items withheld or denied. افسر (helmet), تخت (throne), کلاه (cap/crown‑like headgear) and کمر (here ‘belt / girdle’) are the listed nouns; the repeated و between items is the normal connective. The whole segment enumerates what is not given/granted.
segments.s691-g117: هرآنکس که is a classical relative/indefinite clause marker meaning ‘whoever’ or ‘whomever’; او here is the third‑person pronoun used to refer back emphatically to the person who occupies the throne; برنشیند is the present/subjunctive/past narrative form of نشستن with the prefix بر‑ meaning ‘to sit upon’; بتخت (به + تخت) indicates the place ‘on the throne.’ The clause reads ‘whoever (he is) that sits on the throne.’
segments.s691-g118: خرد (wisdom) stands before باید and functions as the required quality; باید after a noun means ‘is necessary / must be’ (i.e. ‘wisdom is required’); the following و‑coordinations list further necessary qualities: نامداری (renown) and بخت (good fortune / luck). This is a typical Persian nominal + باید construction expressing required attributes.
segments.s691-g119: شناسد is a classical third‑person/formal verb meaning ‘one should know’ or ‘it should be known’; که introduces the content clause ‘that…’; این تخت و این فرهی is a doubled demonstrative ‘this throne and this dignity/glory’ (فرهی = noble dignity/glory) — Persian frequently repeats این for emphasis. The structure is a formulaic ‘know that X…’.
segments.s691-g120: کرا is an archaic interrogative/relative pronoun ‘who / whom’ (used in classical Persian); بود is the past of بودن ‘was’; the coordinating و links the next phrase; دیهیم شاهنشهی here forms a compound identifying the possessive/office — شاهنشهی = ‘the imperial/kingly office’; (دیهیم is an older/rare term and in context functions as part of the nominal phrase meaning the holder/possession of the kingship). Together the clause asks/declares ‘to whom it belonged and who was the holder of the kingship.’
segments.s691-g81: بفرمان is a prepositional phrase meaning "by the order of." آن نامور شهریار is a noun phrase in which آن (demonstrative "that/the") precedes the epithet نامور ("renowned") and the title شهریار ("king"); the whole phrase functions adverbially to mark the agent/authority for the verb that follows.
segments.s691-g82: نهادند is the past, 3rd-person plural of نهادن "to place/set" (they placed). The object follows the verb in this older/classical ordering: کرسی زرین چهار = "four golden seats/thrones," with the numeral چهار postposed after the noun phrase (a common classical Persian order). زرین is an adjective postmodifying کرسی.
segments.s691-g83: نشست here is a noun meaning "sitting/assembly" (not the verb "he sat"). It is followed by the descriptive noun phrase این سه پرمایه نیک رای: این ("these/this") + سه ("three") qualified by پرمایه ("rich/substantial in worth") and the compound adjective نیک رای ("of good counsel"). The modifiers follow the head noun, a typical classical Persian order.
segments.s691-g84: همی‌بود is an imperfective/past-continuous form meaning "continued to be/was remaining." It is followed by the name خراد برزین (here the personal name) and the adverbial بپای (literally "at foot/at the side," meaning "standing/remaining by"); the sequence says that Khurad Barzin remained nearby while the assembly persisted.
segments.s691-g85: بفرمود is an honorific past form meaning here "(he) ordered/commanded/said." قیصر is the subject "Caesar/the emperor." که introduces a subordinate (reported) clause. بر زیرگاه is a prepositional phrase "on the low platform/dais," with بر = "on/upon" and زیرگاه = "lower place/platform."
segments.s691-g86: نشیند is a subjunctive/jussive present used in reported commands after که — here rendered "should sit." Classical Persian often places the verb before its subject for emphasis, so نشیند precedes کسی ("someone/anyone"). کو is a relative particle here meaning "who/that" (introducing the relative clause), and بپیمود is the past participial/ perfect sense "had traversed/ accomplished" modifying راه ("the road").
segments.s691-g87: چنین is an adverb "thus/so." گفت is the past tense verb "said" (classical order can put verb before subject). خراد برزین is the speaker's name; که introduces the reported content clause that follows.
segments.s691-g88: مرا is the first-person object "me" (here split in the token list); را is the definite/object marker attached to the object. در بزرگی = "in/into greatness" (در = "in/into," بزرگی = "greatness"). ندادست is the negated past "did not give" (archaic perfect), and راه ("way/method") here means "a way/entrance" — so the clause says the king did not give me a way into greatness.
segments.s691-g65: نشست is a past‑tense, 3rd‑person singular verb 'he sat' (classical Persian word order often places the verb early). از بر functions as a classical/poetic prepositional cluster meaning 'on/upon' here (از + بر with بر carrying the spatial sense 'on'), followed by the noun phrase نامور تخت عاج 'the renowned ivory throne' (adjective نامور precedes the noun تخت; عاج specifies the material).
segments.s691-g66: به سر is a prepositional phrase 'to/onto the head' (به = 'to/onto', سر = 'head'). برنهاد is a past 3rd‑person singular verb 'placed on/raised up' that takes the preceding prepositional phrase as its goal. آن دل‌افروز تاج is the direct object 'that heart‑delighting crown' (آن = 'that/the', دل‌افروز an adjectival epithet, تاج = 'crown').
segments.s691-g67: بفرمود is an honorific past verb meaning 'he commanded/said' (used of kings). تا introduces a subordinate clause here meaning 'so that/that' (he ordered that ...). پرده is the object 'the curtain'; برداشتند is past 3rd‑person plural 'they removed/lifted'. The object precedes the verb, as is common in Persian.
segments.s691-g68: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. دهلیزشان is a genitive noun 'their vestibule/anteroom' (دهلیز + ـشان). تیز here functions adverbially 'swiftly' (poetic usage), and بگذاشتند is past 3rd‑person plural 'they placed/put' (the object is implicit or recoverable from the context).
segments.s691-g69: This segment strings epithets and a proper name: گرانمایه 'precious/noble' modifies گستهم (here a personal name, Gostaham). بد پیشرو is a compound epithet: بد 'bad/ill' + پیشرو 'forefront/leader' producing an epithet 'ill (or fierce) at the vanguard' — poetic epithets often follow the name in classical Persian.
segments.s691-g70: پس او is 'then he' (پس = then/after, او = he). The following sequence lists companions or epithets joined by و 'and': چوبالوی is a name/epithet (here treated as a proper noun), و is the coordinator 'and', شاپور is the name 'Shapur'. گو at the end is not the finite verb 'say' here but functions as a name‑forming or adjectival element (poetic/epithetual use), so the whole phrase lists persons/titles.
segments.s691-g71: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. The next items are names/agents: خراد and برزین are personal names (or one name in two parts), joined by و 'and'. گرد اندیان is a poetic phrase meaning 'the gathering/assembly of the Andians' (گرد here conveys 'gathering/assembly' with اندیان indicating the people). Together this builds the temporal/agent phrase 'when X and the gathered Andians ...'.
segments.s691-g72: همه is the quantifier 'all/everyone'. The clause that follows lists two parallel items applied to those people: تاج بر سر 'crowns upon (their) heads' and کمر برمیان 'girdles about (their) waists' — بر is the locative 'on/upon', سر = 'head', کمر = 'waist' (here 'girdle/belt'). برمیان in this poetic line functions to indicate placement 'around/about the middle/waist'.
segments.s691-g129: 'چو آن' (here written as چوآن) is a temporal subordinating phrase meaning 'when / as soon as'. 'نامه' is the direct object 'that letter'. 'برخواند' is a past-tense verb 'read / recited aloud' (third person). 'بفزود' is a past-tense causative/augmentative 'increased / caused to grow' and takes 'درد' (sorrow/pain) as its object; the clause therefore means 'when he read that letter, sorrow increased.' Note the poetic word order (verb before object in places) and the causal sequence linking to the next clause.
segments.s691-g130: 'شد' is the past copular/verb 'became' (third person). 'آن تخت' is the subject 'that throne'. 'برچشم او' is a prepositional phrase 'before his eyes'. 'لاژورد' is a predicate nominal 'lapis-lazuli / lapis-blue' describing the throne; the structure is '[the throne] became [lapis-blue] before his eyes.' The order is slightly inverted for poetic effect (predicate after the locative phrase).
segments.s691-g131: The initial item (بخراد) here functions as the speaker (either a proper name or an archaic/poetic form related to 'بخرد'/'wise'); it is followed by the locative/indirect-object phrase 'بر زین جهاندار' ('to/on the saddle, to the sovereign') and ends with the past verb 'گفت' ('said'). Word order places the speaker first, then the phrase identifying to whom he spoke, and finally the reporting verb 'said'.
segments.s691-g132: 'که' introduces the content clause ('that...'). 'این نیست' is the negative copular phrase 'this is not'. The phrase 'برمرد دانا' is an oblique/dative construction meaning 'to/for a (wise) man' (i.e. 'from the point of view of a wise man' / 'hidden from a wise man' depending on idiom). 'نهفت' is a participial/adjectival past form 'hidden, concealed'. Together the clause means 'that this is not hidden from a wise man.' Note the archaic ordering where the participle follows the negated copula.
segments.s691-g133: 'مرا' (here segmented as م + را) is the accusative/topic marker 'me'. 'خسرو' is the subject 'Khosrow'. The comparative phrase 'از خویش و پیوند بیش' means 'more than his own kin and bonds' — 'از' introduces the standard of comparison and 'بیش' indicates 'more.' The line therefore says that Khosrow esteems the speaker above his own relatives and ties; the order reflects poetic inversion (object before subject/phrase).
segments.s691-g134: 'ز جان' is an adverbial phrase 'from the soul / from the heart'. 'سخن گوی' (literally 'speech-speaking') functions as a relative/participial idea 'who speaks words' or 'speaks' (here presenting how he addresses the speaker). 'دارمش' is a first-person imperfect/past compound meaning 'I keep/hold him/it' (the enclitic -مش = 'him/it'), and 'پیش' is 'before / in the presence of / in honour.' Together the clause conveys 'he speaks to me from the heart, and I keep him (before me / in honor).' Note the mixed perspectives (reporting the king's behavior and the speaker's attitude) and the use of enclitic object on 'دارمش'.
segments.s691-g135: This is a triadic apposition: 'سلیح است' ('it is weapon/armament'), followed by correlative 'و هم ... و هم ...' ('and also ... and also ...') listing 'گنج' (treasure) and 'لشکرست' (an army/host). 'لشکرست' is an archaic fused form where 'ست/است' is attached; the whole segment enumerates what is being referred to (arms, treasure, and troops).
segments.s691-g136: 'شما را' marks 'you' as the (topic/oblique) object/emphatic addressee. 'ببین' is an imperative 'see / look'. 'تا' here introduces purpose/result ('so that / in order that / to see') and leads into the subordinate interrogative 'چه اندر خورست' — literally 'what is inside/eaten' but idiomatically 'what will befall / what is in store.' 'خورست' is an archaic verbal/adjectival form related to 'خوردن' used figuratively; the whole means 'Look, you, to see what will befall you' or 'see what awaits you'.
segments.s691-g49: This is a prepositional/relative phrase: 'به چیزی' (toward/for something) + a relative clause 'که برما نیاید شکست' (that defeat does not come upon us). 'برما' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'upon us', and 'نیاید' is the classical negative present/subjunctive form of آمدن used inside a relative clause (‘that does not/must not come’). The whole segment = 'to/for something that will not bring defeat upon us.'
segments.s691-g50: 'بکوشید' is a plural/polite imperative or jussive (بـ + کوشش) meaning 'strive/try'. The conjunctor 'و' links the clauses. 'با آن بسایید دست' literally 'with that rub/use the hand' — idiomatically 'apply your hand to it' or 'make use of it'; 'با آن' (with that) precedes the verb 'بسایید' (here used as an exhortation 'make it serve / employ it'), and 'دست' is the literal noun 'hand' used in the idiom meaning 'work/effort'.
segments.s691-g51: 'تو' (you) is the vocative/subject. 'پیمان گفتار من' is a genitive chain 'the pledge/covenant of my speech' (noun + possession), with 'من' postposed as possessive. 'در پذیر' (در + پذیر → درپذیر) is an adjectival/participial predicate meaning 'be receptive/accepting'; here it functions as an exhortation directed to 'تو'. Word order places the noun phrase before the predicate adjective.
segments.s691-g52: 'سخن هرچ گفتم' = 'the words (saying) whatever I said' — 'هرچ' introduces a free relative ('whatever'), and 'گفتم' is the first‑person past ('I said') forming the relative clause. 'همه' quantifies ('all'), and 'یادگیر' is an imperative form 'learn/remember' (classical short-form imperative: یادگیر rather than the modern two-word form). The sequence is an instruction: 'Learn/remember all the words that I have said.'
segments.s691-g53: 'شنیدند' is past tense, 3rd person plural ('they heard'). The following noun phrase 'آواز فرخ جوان' is a possessive/attributive chain: 'the voice/song of the fortunate youth' (adjective 'فرخ' precedes the head noun 'جوان'). The verb precedes its object here (a common poetic word order: V + object).
segments.s691-g54: This string contains two adjectival epithets modifying the noun 'گردان' (here 'company', 'troop', or 'assembly'): 'جهاندیده' ('worldly-wise') and the compound adjective 'روشن‌روان' ('bright‑souled' / cheerful). In classical Persian multiple adjectives can precede the head noun; together they form the subject of the preceding verb 'شنیدند' (i.e. 'the worldly‑wise, bright‑souled company').
segments.s691-g55: 'همه خواندند آفرین سر به سر' — 'همه' (all/everyone) is the subject; 'خواندند' (they uttered/sang/called) is past 3rd plural used for making an acclamation; 'آفرین' is an interjection ('bravo'/'well done'). The idiom 'سر به سر' here means 'one after another' or 'each in turn' (a fixed phrase), so the whole means 'they all exclaimed
segments.s691-g56: 'که' introduces the content of their acclamation ('that...'). 'جز تو' = 'except you'. 'مبادا' is an optative/negative wish ('may it not be' / 'let there not be'); when combined with 'کسی' it forms 'مبادا کسی' = 'may there be no one'. The clause 'کسی تاجور' places the predicate noun 'تاجور' after 'کسی' (i.e. 'anyone be a تاجور'). Grammatically this is an optative clause meaning 'may no one except you be X.' (In older Persian texts 'تاجور' can carry an archaic sense beyond a literal 'merchant'.)
segments.s691-g25: خردمند باشید is an imperative-plural construction: خردمند (adjective ‘wise’) comes before باشید, the present-subjunctive/imperative form of بودن used here as ‘be’ (second-person plural polite). روشن روان is a two-word descriptive phrase modifying the same subject: روشن (= bright/clear) qualifies روان (lit. ‘soul/mind’), so together ‘clear-minded’ or ‘of clear spirit.’ The adjectival phrases are coordinated by the implied و linking to the next segment.
segments.s691-g26: نیوشنده is a noun/participle meaning ‘listener’ or ‘one who hears,’ here used as a desirable quality (‘be listeners’). و is the coordinating conjunction ‘and.’ چرب و شیرین زبان is a compound nominal phrase: چرب ‘smooth/ flattering’ and شیرین ‘sweet’ both modify زبان ‘tongue/speech’, yielding ‘smooth- and sweet‑tongued’ (i.e. flattering or pleasant in speech). Word order places adjectives before the head noun, a normal Persian pattern in poetic style.
segments.s691-g27: گر here introduces a conditional (‘if’). ای دون addresses the addressees as ‘O base/ignoble ones’ (دون as a vocative/characterisation). که begins a subordinate clause ‘that/when’, and قیصر به میدان شود is the clause’s predicate: قیصر ‘Caesar’ + به میدان ‘to the field/arena’ + شود (شد in subjunctive/indicative) ‘goes/enters/comes to be’ — together ‘when the Caesar goes out to the field.’
segments.s691-g28: کمان خواهد states the main clause: کمان ‘a bow’ as subject and خواهد (from خواست) in the sense of future/modal ‘will be desired/wished for.’ ار is the conditional ‘if/when’ introducing the condition that follows: نی به چوگان شود ‘if a reed (نی) is turned into/used as (به) a چوگان (polo‑mallet)’ with شود ‘becomes’ — literally ‘a bow will be desired if the reed becomes a mallet.’ The sentence uses two conditionals to make a proverbial point.
segments.s691-g29: بکوشید is the second‑person plural imperative/subjunctive of کوشیدن ‘to strive/try’: ‘strive’ or ‘make an effort.’ با + مرد is the prepositional object ‘with a man,’ and خسروپرست (a compound) describes that man as ‘a worshipper/adorer of the king’ (i.e. a pro‑royalist or flatterer). The whole segment is an instruction to ally or act together with such a man.
segments.s691-g30: This segment splits the verb بدَان (ب + دان) 'know this' into prefix and root: ب (here the prefix forming the imperative/infinitive) + دان (to know/consider) = ‘know/consider.’ تا introduces purpose/result ‘so that/so as to,’ and شما را is the experiencer/dative marked with را (emphatic/definite object marker): literally ‘to you.’ نیاید شکست is a negative-purpose clause with نیاید (negative present subjunctive of آمدن) ‘may not come’ and شکست ‘defeat’ as the thing that must not come upon you — ‘so that defeat may not come to you.’ Note Persian word order places شما را before the verb and شکست at the end.
segments.s691-g31: سواری بداند is a general statement: سواری ‘a rider/horseman’ + بداند (3rd‑person present/subjunctive of دانستن) meaning ‘should know’ or ‘let him know.’ کز introduces the content clause ‘that/from which’ (here functioning as ‘that’ or ‘that from’), followed by ایران as the source and برند (3rd‑person plural of بردن) ‘they take/carry’ — so the clause means ‘know that from Iran they take…’
segments.s691-g32: دلیری و نیرو are the coordinated nouns ‘bravery (daring) and power/strength.’ ز (poetic variant of از) is the preposition ‘from,’ and شیران is the plural noun ‘lions’ (used here literally or metaphorically for courageous men). برند repeats the verb ‘they take/carry away.’ Together: ‘they take bravery and strength from lions.’
segments.s691-g1: چوآمد is the fused poetic form of چو + آمد, a temporal clause meaning "when (he) came." بران is بر + آن (poetic contraction) meaning "to/on that" and takes the following noun شارستان ("city/town") as its complement. شهریار is the subject "the king" placed after the locative phrase; overall the clause means "when the king came to that town."
segments.s691-g2: سوار is a noun here, "a horseman/rider." آمد is the past tense verb "came." از introduces the source/agent "from," and قیصر نامدار is a postpositive adjective construction: قیصر ("Caesar") qualified by نامدار ("renowned"). The phrase means "a horseman came from the renowned Caesar."
segments.s691-g3: که introduces a subordinate/conditional clause ("if/that"). چیزی "anything/something" is the thing referred to; کزین is a poetic contraction of از + این meaning "from this" and modifies مرز ("border/frontier"). باید is the modal "must/ought," and بخواه is the present-subjunctive/infinitive meaning "seek/ask." The sequence expresses "if anything must be sought from this border."
segments.s691-g4: مدار is used figuratively as a noun meaning "sphere/centre/realm" (poetic usage). آرزو را uses را as the definite/object marker marking "the desire/hope" as the theme. ز شاهان is a prepositional phrase "from/of kings" showing the domain referenced. نگاه (here) means "view/consideration" or more loosely "regard/attention." The clause literally advises not to make the sphere/centre of your hope be among the kings—i.e. do not set your hopes upon kings.
segments.s691-g5: که here begins a concessive clause; هرچند is the concessive adverb "although." این پادشاهی is the noun phrase "this kingship/sovereignty." مراست is a poetic contraction of مرا + است ("is mine"); it functions predicatively to mean "belongs to me" or "is mine."
segments.s691-g6: تو را is the object marked by را ("you"). با تن خویش is a prepositional phrase "with our own body/person" where خویش is reflexive/"one's own" (here split as خوی + ش). داریم is the present plural "we have/hold." راست here functions as an adverb "true/indeed," strengthening the assertion. Word order (object before verb) is normal Persian.
segments.s691-g7: بران = بر + آن ("on/at that"), here locating the address in "that town" (شارستان). ایمن و شاد are coordinate adjectives "safe and glad/happy." باش is the second-person imperative "be!" giving the benediction/address.
segments.s691-g8: ز هر بد is a prepositional phrase "from/away from every evil/misfortune." که introduces the relative clause "that/which" and اندیشی is the second-person present/subjunctive of "to think" ("you may think/you think"). آزاد باش is an imperative phrase "be free/be independent." The whole means "be free from every ill that you might think of."
segments.s691-g105: چنان تا introduces a temporal clause: چنان (an adverb 'thus') + تا ('until') marks the limit in time; بشاه is the prepositional phrase with the prefix be- meaning 'to the king' (بشاه = 'to/at the king'), آفریدون is the king's name, and رسید is the simple past 'arrived/reached'. The whole segment means 'thus, until it reached King Afrīdūn.' Note the archaic poetic word-order where the goal (بشاه آفریدون) follows تا and precedes the verb رسید.
segments.s691-g106: کزان is a compound prepositional phrase ('from which' / 'from among them'); سرفرازان is the plural noun 'the distinguished/glorious ones'; و here functions as a coordinating particle ('and') or simple connector preserved in the text; را is the definite-object marker attaching to the object; برگزید is the past-tense verb 'chose/selected'. Word order is source → object(+را) → verb, typical in Persian.
segments.s691-g107: پدید آمد is a two-part verbal expression meaning 'appeared/manifested' (پدید 'appear' + آمد 'came'); آن تخمه is the noun phrase 'that seed/offspring' (تخمه used metaphorically as 'lineage' or 'stock'); اندرجهان is the locative 'in the world'. The clause reads literally 'That offspring appeared in the world.'
segments.s691-g108: This is a balanced/past statement: ببود is an archaic past copula 'was/then became'; آشکار 'clear/openly' qualifies the verb; آنچ is a relative 'that which/what'; بودی is a past form 'had been' (here marking the prior hidden state); نهان 'hidden'. Together they mean 'that which had been hidden became manifest.' Note the inverted, poetic order (broadly: became-manifest that-which had-been hidden).
segments.s691-g109: همی‌رو چنین is a narrative-progressive phrase 'it kept going thus/it continued in this way' (همی- marking ongoing action). تا introduces the temporal limit 'until'; سر here ('head') is used with the following name to mark the person affected, and کی + قباد together form the royal name 'Kay Qobad' (in the text کی is a title). The phrase functions as 'until (the time of) Kay Qobad.'
segments.s691-g110: که introduces a relative/temporal clause 'when/that'; تاج بزرگی is the noun phrase 'a great crown' (تاج 'crown' modified by بزرگی 'greatness/size'); به سر is a locative phrase 'on the head' (به marking placement) and برنهاد is the past verb 'placed on/put upon'. The clause means 'when a great crown was placed on (his) head.'
segments.s691-g111: نیامد is the past negative 'did not come' or 'did not befall'; the preposition ب (here fused with the following noun) + دین yields 'to/into this faith/religion' (بدین دین = 'to this religion'); هرگز بدی 'evil never' is the negated object phrase. The clause says 'evil never came to/ befell this religion.'
segments.s691-g112: نگه داشتندی is an archaic past plural 'they kept/maintained' (نگه here 'keeping' or part of the compound, داشتندی = 'they held/kept'); ره ایزدی is the object 'the divine way/path' (ره 'way, course' + ایزدی 'divine'). Together: 'they observed/kept the divine path.'
segments.s691-g41: چونامه بخواند is a temporal clause: چونامه (an older/poetic word for a letter or tale) + بخواند (third-person singular 'reads/recites' in a poetic narrative form). زبان برگشای is an imperative instruction placed after the clause: زبان ('tongue' = 'speech') followed by برگشای (archaic imperative/subjunctive 'open' = 'speak'). The word order is inverted for emphasis (verb-final temporal clause, then vocative/imperative).
segments.s691-g42: به گفتار با تو ندارند پای is an idiomatic negative statement. به گفتار ('in speech / in argument') sets the domain; با تو ('with you') marks the opponent; ندارند (present plural 'they do not have') negates possession; پای ('foot/foothold') is used figuratively — 'to have a foothold/standing'. The clause means 'they have no standing against you in argument.'
segments.s691-g43: ببالوی is an archaic/prepositional particle meaning 'beside/alongside' or 'moreover'. گفت here functions as the noun 'speech/discourse' (an elliptical relative construction): آنچ ('that which') + قیصر ز من ('the Caesar from me' = 'the Caesar had told me / to me') yields 'that which the Caesar said to me' — a compressed relative clause common in poetry (omitted که/از → ز).
segments.s691-g44: گشاید زبان بر سرانجمن: گشاید (from گشاد) is a poetic verb-form meaning 'open' or 'unfold' (here 'opened/speaks'); زبان ('tongue' = speech) is the thing opened; بر + سرانجمن ('before/on the leaders of the assembly') indicates the audience. The verbal phrase 'زبان گشودن' is idiomatic for 'to begin to speak.'
segments.s691-g45: ز governs a list of moral/legal items: ز ('from/of') + فرمان (command) و سوگند (and oath) و پیمان (and covenant) و عهد (and pledge). The preposition z groups these obligations together as the things to be kept in mind or spoken of.
segments.s691-g46: تو اندر سخن یاد کن همچو شهد is a direct imperative addressed to 'you' (تو). اندر سخن ('in speech/among words') sets the domain; یاد کن is the imperative 'remember/mention' (یاد + فعل کن); همچو شهد is a simile 'like honey'—i.e., remember or present these matters sweetly/pleasantly in your speech. The clause uses archaic inward preposition اندر and the simple 2nd-person imperative کن.
segments.s691-g47: بدان انجمن تو زبان منی is compressed, archaic phrasing: بدان (a contraction like به + آن = 'in/to that') + انجمن ('assembly') gives 'in that assembly'. The predicate is inverted: تو زبان منی literally 'you (are) my tongue' — زبان used metaphorically for 'voice/spokesperson' and ی is the archaic copula marking 'are'. Word order (locative phrase first, then predicate) is typical of poetry.
segments.s691-g48: بهر نیک و بد ترجمان منی: بهر ('for/regarding') + نیک و بد ('good and bad') marks the sphere; ترجمان ('interpreter/translator') names the role; منی (من + ی) = 'you are mine' / 'you are my ...' with the copula y. The clause means 'you act as my interpreter in matters both good and bad.'

sentences.s691-s69: If you want to see, I have no reluctance; for sight is better than treasure, dinars, and the sword.
sentences.s691-s45: That I took my seat before Caesar, and thus the letter of the King of Iran came into his hand.
sentences.s691-s46: Unless you accept servitude, you will not be approved; by his message you will be useful.
sentences.s691-s47: Caesar said to him, 'Unveil the secret; what did that proud wise man say?'
sentences.s691-s48: First he uttered praise for the sovereign; by that praise he humbled the world.
sentences.s691-s9: To the wise Borzin and Shapur-Shīr the brave king thus spoke.
sentences.s691-s10: When the horses become bright, make saddles; on top of that place a golden saddle.
sentences.s691-s11: Put on brocaded Chinese robes; be all of one heart and of pure counsel.
sentences.s691-s12: From this town go toward the Caesar; speak (to him) and listen to his words.
sentences.s691-s37: They drew near the exalted Caesar; when they saw him, they brought their prayers before him.
sentences.s691-s38: All with one voice cried 'Bravo!' and scattered gold and jewels upon that throne.
sentences.s691-s39: First Caesar asked the king about Iran, and about the army and the hardships of the road.
sentences.s691-s40: When Kharad Barzin heard, he went up to the throne with the king's letter.
sentences.s691-s29: They turned their faces toward the Caesar — the great men, bright‑hearted and truthful of speech.
sentences.s691-s30: When the Caesar heard that from Iran noble visitors had been sent by the sovereign of the world.
sentences.s691-s31: They came up to the portico by the road; he sent several troops to receive them.
sentences.s691-s32: He adorned a palace in Roman brocade; all its figure was jewels and gold of the realm.
sentences.s691-s61: Help me in this matter; bring success against this faithless one.
sentences.s691-s62: For we have become wanderers about the world; we humans have come from ancient times and (even) the moon.
sentences.s691-s63: When Caesar heard such words, he was struck with ruin and became like the fenugreek-flower.
sentences.s691-s64: The fenugreek-flower of it filled with dew; his tongue and soul became full of lament.
sentences.s691-s5: All the Romans are altogether inferior to you, though a few are headstrong and claim to be greater.
sentences.s691-s6: I will not seek food, sleep, or a place of rest until I have made you arms and an army.
sentences.s691-s7: When the king heard that, he became joyful; his mind was freed from anxiety.
sentences.s691-s8: He commanded Gostaham and Baluy — those very world-seeking men.
sentences.s691-s17: The king ordered Barzin to buy Chinese silks and black musk.
sentences.s691-s18: A letter must be written to Caesar; I will buy paradise like the shining sun.
sentences.s691-s19: Brief words and abundant meaning, that remembrance may seize the heart of every person.
sentences.s691-s20: That philosophers be near him; strive at that so that they do not hear any nonsense.
sentences.s691-s49: who is (indeed) superior — above venom — powerful and knowing in every domain.
sentences.s691-s50: By his command this heaven turns; how (or where) is it superior to place and time?
sentences.s691-s51: The heavens and the stars have all acted thus; they have raised (or set) it upon this revolving wheel.
sentences.s691-s52: When from the earth he made me a living creature, he first brought Kiumars to life.
sentences.s691-s57: Now a foul‑mouthed servant came, and moreover sat upon the throne of the Kayanids.
sentences.s691-s58: I will not give (or grant) from the oppressor — neither helmet, nor throne, nor cap nor belt.
sentences.s691-s59: Whoever sits on that throne must have wisdom, renown, and good fortune.
sentences.s691-s60: One should know to whom this throne and this dignity belonged, and who was the holder of the royal sovereignty.
sentences.s691-s41: By the command of that renowned king they set four golden thrones.
sentences.s691-s42: This sitting/assembly of the three, rich in merit and good counsel, continued, Khurad Barzin remaining at their side.
sentences.s691-s43: The Caesar commanded that on the low platform should sit anyone who had traversed the road.
sentences.s691-s44: Thus Khurad Barzin said that the king had given me no way into greatness.
sentences.s691-s33: He sat upon the renowned ivory throne and set that heart-delighting crown upon his head.
sentences.s691-s34: He commanded that the curtain be lifted; they removed the curtain and, from their vestibule, quickly set it down / moved swiftly.
sentences.s691-s35: The noble Gostaham, ill at the forefront; then he — Chubalu (or Chubaluy) and Shapur‑(called) —
sentences.s691-s36: When Kharad, Barzin, and the gathered Andians all put crowns on their heads and girdles round their waists.
sentences.s691-s65: When he read that letter his sorrow grew; the throne before his eyes turned lapis-blue.
sentences.s691-s66: He (or Bakh-rad) spoke to the sovereign from the saddle, saying, 'This is not hidden from a wise man.'
sentences.s691-s67: Khosrow regards me more than his own kin and ties; he speaks to me from the heart and I keep him before me.
sentences.s691-s68: It is arms, and also treasure, and also an army; look, you, to see what will befall you.
sentences.s691-s25: Strive for whatever will not bring defeat upon us, and with that put your hand to work.
sentences.s691-s26: Accept my pledge of speech and be receptive to my words; learn (or remember) all that I have said.
sentences.s691-s27: The worldly-wise, bright-souled company heard the voice of the fortunate young man.
sentences.s691-s28: All of them cried 'Afarin!' one by one, saying, 'May there be no one but you who is a tyrant/tyrant-like (tājūr).'
sentences.s691-s13: Be wise and clear-minded, attentive listeners, and smooth and sweet of tongue.
sentences.s691-s14: If (you are) base — when the Caesar goes out to the field — a bow will be wanted if a reed is turned into a polo‑mallet.
sentences.s691-s15: Make an effort with a man who loves the king; know this, so that defeat may not come upon you.
sentences.s691-s16: A horseman should know that from Iran they take bravery and strength from lions.
sentences.s691-s1: When the king came to that town, a horseman came from the renowned Caesar.
sentences.s691-s2: If there is anything you should ask from this border, do not place your hopes in the kings' sphere (i.e. do not set your hopes upon kings).
sentences.s691-s3: Although this kingship is mine, we hold you truly as (close as) our own body.
sentences.s691-s4: Be safe and joyful in that town; be free from every ill that you might think of.
sentences.s691-s53: Thus it went until it reached King Afrīdūn, from whom he selected the distinguished ones.
sentences.s691-s54: That stock appeared in the world; what had been hidden became manifest.
sentences.s691-s55: It went on thus until the time of Kay Qobad, when a great crown was set upon his head.
sentences.s691-s56: Evil never came upon this faith; they observed the divine path.
sentences.s691-s21: When he reads a tale (or letter), open your tongue; in speech they have no foothold against you.
sentences.s691-s22: Besides that, he related what the Caesar had said to me and opened his tongue before the assembly.
sentences.s691-s23: In your speech remember your command, oath, covenant, and pledge, as if (they were) honey.
sentences.s691-s24: In that assembly you are my tongue; for good and ill you are my interpreter.
